commit.message:
Prioritize image-capable backend pixel dimensions in MultiScreen and add fall back pseudo image on multibackend (#119)

* Initial plan

* Prioritize image-supporting backend pixel dimensions in MultiScreen

When multiple backends are connected via MultiScreen, the text cell
dimensions in pixels (getTextWidth/getTextHeight) now prioritize
screens whose backend supports an image protocol (e.g. sixel or
CSI 16t). This avoids black lines when a non-image terminal with
incorrect default pixel dimensions is connected alongside an image-
capable terminal.

If no backend supports images, the behavior falls back to the
previous minimum-across-all-screens logic.

commit.message:
fix: 429 status bar flashing, UI lockup, and error logging

Three bugs fixed:

1. Status bar 'flashing': The spinner timer (100ms) was overwriting
   the rate-limit countdown text with 'Thinking...' immediately.
   Added _custom_activity field that persists across spinner ticks
   until a state transition clears it.

2. UI lockup during retry: The countdown loop now checks
   self.is_streaming each second and returns early if the user
   quits. action_quit sets is_streaming=False to break the loop.

3. Error logging: 429 errors now logged at ERROR level via
   log_api_error() with the full Azure error message (which
   reveals whether it's RPM vs TPM limit), in addition to the
   INFO-level retry event.

commit.message:
fix: stage calamares overlay in /root to avoid package conflict

archiso copies iso/airootfs/ into the work rootfs BEFORE running pacstrap,
so any file that the calamares package also provides (e.g. netinstall.yaml)
causes a 'conflicting files' transaction error.

Stage the calamares config in /root/calamares-overlay/ instead, then
copy it into /etc/calamares/ inside customize_airootfs.sh which runs
after package installation.

commit.message:
Fix: use retry.RetryOnConflict to prevent retry storm on composition status updates (#574)

When multiple controllers concurrently update composition status,
conflict
errors cause immediate requeues without backoff, leading to a retry
storm
that can overwhelm the API server (142+ retries/second observed).

This fix uses k8s.io/client-go/util/retry.RetryOnConflict which:
- Re-fetches the composition before each retry attempt
- Applies exponential backoff between retries
- Handles conflicts internally instead of propagating errors

This prevents the thundering herd effect where conflict errors cascade
through the controller-runtime work queue.

---------

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Enable `/agent agentic-workflows debug` handling in Issue Triage workflow (#3019)

The issue triage agent workflow could not be triggered via the `/agent
...` command path for workflow debugging. This change wires the workflow
to accept `/agent agentic-workflows debug` and gives the agent the
minimal read access/tools required to inspect workflow failures.

- **Trigger surface update**
- Added `on.slash_command` to the agentic workflow source so issue
comments with `/agent` can invoke the workflow.
  - Scoped to `issue_comment` events.

- **Workflow-debug capability enablement**
- Added `permissions.actions: read` so the agent can read
workflow/job/run metadata.
  - Enabled `tools.agentic-workflows`.
- Expanded GitHub MCP toolsets to include `actions` alongside existing
`issues`/`labels`.

- **Prompt behavior update**
- Added an explicit goal line instructing the agent to handle `/agent
agentic-workflows debug` by summarizing recent workflow failures in a
concise comment.

```yaml
on:
  issues:
    types: [opened]
  slash_command:
    name: agent
    events: [issue_comment]

permissions:
  issues: read
  actions: read

tools:
```

commit.message:
[bug-fix] azd update: fix SIGKILL during binary replacement on macOS (#7012)

* fix: remove binary before replacement to prevent SIGKILL on macOS

On macOS, copyFile used os.Create(dst) which truncates the running binary
in place (same inode). This corrupts memory-mapped code pages and causes
the OS to SIGKILL the process mid-update, leaving a broken binary.

Fix: on unix, always os.Remove(dst) before os.Create(dst). This creates
a new inode — the OS keeps the old inode alive for the running process
via its fd. Also simplifies the previous Linux-only ETXTBSY workaround
into a single remove-first path for all unix platforms.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

syscall.Stat_t is undefined on Windows. Move

Merge Experiment branch: allocation optimizations and benchmark fix

Brings in three performance improvements and one benchmark correctness fix:

1. NativeMemory.AlignedRealloc for buffer growth (NativeAllocator.cs)
   NativeBuffer.Grow() now extends in-place via AlignedRealloc instead of
   alloc + memcpy + free. Benefits BYTE_ARRAY columns with many pages.

2. ArrayPool for definition/repetition level buffers (ArrowArrayBuilder.cs)
   _defLevels and _repLevels switched from NativeBuffer<int> to
   ArrayPool<int>.Shared. Eliminates two native alloc/free calls per
   nullable column read.

3. In-place reverse scatter for nullable fixed-width columns (ArrowArrayBuilder.cs)
   BuildFixedArray<T> and BuildDenseFixedArray<T> no longer allocate a
   second scatter buffer. Dense values are scattered right-to-left within
   the existing value buffer (safe because non-null values only move
   rightward). The validity bitmap switches from NativeBuffer<byte> to
   MemoryPool<byte>.Shared (no alignment needed for bitmaps). Eliminates
   1 AlignedAlloc + 1 AlignedFree per nullable fixed-width column.

4. Fix Parquet.Net benchmark to materialize Data (RowGroupReadBenchmarks.cs)
   Previously .Data was never accessed after ReadColumnAsync, so the lazy
   scatter/unpack of definition levels was never triggered. Now touches
   .Data.Length to force UnpackDefinitions — making the comparison
   equivalent to what EW produces eagerly.

commit.message:
feat: introduce BmwContext, BmwRequest, and route jump table (fixes #922)

Introduce the BMW request context abstraction and O(1) route dispatch:

BmwContext (Core) — lightweight request context created once per request.
Resolves Kestrel IFeatureCollection features exactly once (response,
connection, body pipes). Holds BmwRequest, source IP, route parameters,
and PageInfo. HttpContext remains accessible for migration compatibility.

BmwRequest (Core) — readonly struct with Method, Path, QueryString,
and pre-computed RouteKey ("METHOD /path") for jump-table lookup.

RouteHash (Host) — FNV-1a hash function operating on string and
ReadOnlySpan<char>. Deterministic, branchless inner loop.

RouteJumpTable (Host) — open-addressing hash table with linear probing.
Only indexes exact-match routes (no parameters/regex/catch-all).
Load factor ≤ 0.5 for fast probing. Rebuilt lazily when routes change.

RequestHandler updated to:
- Create BmwContext once at pipeline entry (feature resolution once)
- Use jump table as primary O(1) fast path for exact-match routes
- Fall through to pattern matching for parameterised routes
- Eliminate redundant string concatenation for route key

[ios] not calling activation callback on inactive gestures (#3986)

## Description

Gestures always call activation callback upon finalizing, regardless of
the previous state. This is intended for normal gestures, however for
manually handled gestures we don't want to call it. This PR unifies the
behaviour across platforms, GestureManager.finalize() won't call
activation callback.

commit.message:
Reduce custom operator behavior in the Volatile<T> structure (#124244)

- None of the logic changed here is actually significant. The only
actual bug here is the behavior of `Module::SetDebuggerInfoBits`, and
`Module::EnableEditAndContinue` which now use interlocked operations for
situations that can theoretically race, due to the behavior of
Reflection.Emit.
- The PAL changes are to remove marking fields which are always
protected with a critical section for using volatile
- The GC changes are mostly around alloc_context_count which has
interesting behavior. It is intentionally written using racy logic which
is approximately correct. I'm hesitant to actually change the behavior
here without much more extensive testing, but we may want to consider
whether or not the increased re-order buffer lengths and whatnot in
modern CPUS make it more important to consider using atomic
increment/decrement here.

These are most of the non-rote changes extracted from #124106, for
independent review/merge.
